Article Title: Amid rising chip prices, tech companies may have new ‘headache’ and the reason is US-Israel-Iran war
Article Text:
Impact on the tech giants



Iran war hits Trump’s AI chip export push







The global technology companies are already facing a lot of challenges with the increasing cost of semiconductor but now it appears that they may face a deeper crises as the US-Israel-Iran war disrupts important supply chains. According to a report by Reuters, the conflict has started to affect the flow of circuit boards and raw materials, increasing costs and creating fresh uncertainty for manufactures around the globe. The strategic location of Iran along key shipping routes has made the war particularly damaging for the electronics industry. The ongoing war has disrupted the movement of circuit boards which is an important component for smartphones, laptops and servers. As per the Reuters report, this disruption is forcing the companies to look for alternative supplies at higher prices.Analysts also warn that this could lead to production delays and increased customer costs, computing the challenges already posed by inflation and high demand for AI-drive hardware.Major tech players such as Apple, Dell and HP are reportedly monitoring the situation closely as supply chain bottlenecks threaten their ability to meet delivery schedules.The report by Reuters further add that smaller companies which lack diversified sourcing strategies, may be hit even harder. The war has also raised concerns about long-term stability in the Middle East, a region critical to global energy and shipping networks.Experts suggest that if the conflict continues, tech companies may need to restructure supply chains, shifting production to regions less exposed to geopolitical risk. However, such moves could take months or even years, leaving firms vulnerable in the short term.Earlier this month, it was reported that Iran war has given a blow to Trump’s AI chip export push. President Donald Trump came to office promising to make America the undisputed leader in artificial intelligence. But the small government office responsible for approving the export of American chips and technology to the world is buckling — and his own administration's policy choices may be making it worse. According to a Bloomberg News investigation, the Bureau of Industry and Security, housed within the Commerce Department, is facing a combination of staff departures, lengthy approval delays, and shifting policy direction at precisely the moment it is needed most.Bloomberg reports that export licence approvals for chipmakers and technology companies have stretched to several months, creating multi-billion dollar backlogs — including for shipments bound for close US allies. Citing data compiled by the Semiconductor Industry Association from members including Intel, AMD and ASML, the report says that in the first half of 2025, licences for chip exports to allies such as Canada, Japan and the United Kingdom took an average of 76 days — roughly double the 38-day average recorded in 2023.
Article Summary: The conflict has started to affect the flow of circuit boards and raw materials, increasing costs and creating fresh uncertainty for manufactures around the globe. The strategic location of Iran along key shipping routes has made the war particularly damaging for the electronics industry. The war has also raised concerns about long-term stability in the Middle East.

Article Title: US directs diplomats to flag risks of Chinese AI models using American tech: Report
Article Text:
What cable to US diplomats across world says

China rejects allegations

Poll What is your opinion on using AI models distilled from proprietary systems? It's unethical It's acceptable if publicly sourced It's a common practice





The US State Department has asked its diplomats worldwide to raise concerns about what it says are efforts by Chinese companies, including AI ‌startup DeepSeek, to steal intellectual property from American AI labs, reports Reuters. Citing diplomatic cable, the report says diplomats have been instructed to speak to their foreign counterparts about "concerns over adversaries' extraction and distillation of U.S. A.I. models." Last week, the White House made similar accusations against Chinese companies, directing US counterpoints to explore measures to stop China from stealing US AI technology.The cable is dated April 24 and has been reportedly sent to diplomatic and consular posts around the world. It referred to companies including DeepSeek and mentioned other firms such as Moonshot AI and MiniMax.The cable stated that a separate message had also been sent to Beijing to raise the issue with Chinese authorities. "A separate demarche request and message has been sent to Beijing for raising with China," the document states.For those unaware, distillation refers to training smaller AI systems using outputs from larger models to reduce costs.The State Department cable said its purpose was to "warn of the risks ⁠of utilizing AI models distilled from U.S. proprietary AI models, and lay the groundwork for potential follow-up and outreach by the U.S. government."The cable said that "AI models developed from surreptitious, unauthorized distillation campaigns enable foreign actors to ⁠release products that appear to perform comparably on select benchmarks at a fraction of the cost but do not replicate the full performance of the original system." It further added that the campaigns also "deliberately strip security protocols from the resulting models and undo mechanisms that ensure those AI models are ideologically ⁠neutral and truth-seeking. "China has denied the claims. The Chinese Embassy in Washington said: “The allegations that Chinese entities are stealing American AI intellectual property are groundless and are deliberate attacks on China's development and progress in the AI industry,” according to Reuters.DeepSeek has previously said its models are trained using publicly available data and web-based sources.
Article Summary: US State Department asks its diplomats worldwide to raise concerns about what it says are efforts by Chinese companies to steal intellectual property from American AI labs. Last week, the White House made similar accusations against Chinese companies, directing US counterpoints to explore measures to stop China from stealing US AI technology.

When John Ternus officially takes over as Apple ’s CEO on September 1, he will be launching a string of products – a number which is said to be more than thrice as compared to what the Cupertino-based giant debuted under Tim Cook (Apple Watch, AirPods, and Vision Pro) during his 15-year tenure. According to a report by Bloomberg’s Mark Gurman, Ternus is poised to oversee the debut of roughly 10 new product categories in the coming years in multiple categories.Less than two weeks after stepping into the role, Ternus is expected to host the unveiling of the first-ever foldable iPhone, which is poised to instantly become the face of Apple’s next big chapter. Priced at an expected $2,000 or more, the smartphone is designed to boost revenue and entice Android users who have used foldables for years.Under Ternus's direction, Apple focused on four key areas to beat the competition, including durability of the folding mechanism, invisible crease, iPad-style experience and peak performance.Gurman says that during a recent company town hall, Ternus told employees that Apple is about to “change the world” again, calling the current pipeline the most exciting of his career. Beyond the foldable phone, here are the nine other categories expected to shape his legacy:Apple is moving into the home to challenge Ring and Nest. This includes a(a HomePod with a screen), afeaturing a screen on a moving robotic arm for better video calls, and a newApple is said to be developing “camera-first” wearables to feed data into its AI systems. These includeto compete with Meta’s Ray-Bans,to give Siri “eyes”, and athat can be worn as a necklace or pinned to a shirt.The “Ternus Era” will likely see the firstin late 2026. Further out, Apple is aiming for a lightweight(targeted for 2028-2030) that could eventually replace the iPhone entirely. A 20-inchis also in development, the report noted.While Ternus is a hardware master, he must now align the entire company, specifically the software teams, to fix Siri and ensure the AI “brains” match the purported new hardware lineup.
Article Summary: John Ternus officially takes over as Apple ’s CEO on September 1. He is expected to host the unveiling of the first-ever foldable iPhone. Apple is said to be developing “camera-first” wearables to feed data into its AI systems.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Mark Zuckerberg for success: “Move fast and break things. Unless you are breaking stuff, you are not moving fast enough.”
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Mark Zuckerberg for success

Quote of the day by Mark Zuckerberg for success

“Move fast and break things. Unless you are breaking stuff, you are not moving fast enough.”

What is the meaning of the quote by Mark Zuckerberg

How this mindset shaped the tech industry

The role of failure in progress

Why speed matters in a changing world

How this idea applies beyond technology

A shift in thinking over time

Other famous quotes by Mark Zuckerberg

“In a world that’s changing really quickly, the only strategy that is guaranteed to fail is not taking risks.”

“The question isn’t ‘What do we want to know about people?’ It’s, ‘What do people want to tell about themselves?”

“This is a perverse thing, personally, but I would rather be in the cycle where people are underestimating us. It gives us the latitude to go out and make big bets that excite and amaze people.”

“People can be really smart or have skills that are directly applicable, but if they don’t really believe in it, then they are not going to really work hard.”

“People don’t care about what someone says about you in a movie–or even what you say, right? They care about what you build.”

Why this quote continues to stay relevant

Why taking risks and learning from mistakes drives long-term success

Poll Do you believe that taking risks can lead to innovation and growth? Yes, absolutely No, it's too risky





"Move fast and break things" is Mark Zuckerberg 's quote of the day. One of the most talked-about ideas in the age of modern technology is "Unless you are breaking stuff, you are not moving fast enough.” People often connect it to new ideas, new businesses, and the rapid growth of digital platforms. At first, the statement might sound mean or even dangerous. But on a larger scale, it shows a way of thinking that values speed, trying new things, and learning by doing.This quote shows that progress often means trying new things before they are perfect. This is especially true in a world where business, technology, and communication are all changing very quickly. It says that making mistakes is a normal part of creating something new. Over time, this method has changed not only the tech industry but also how people think about learning, growing, and being successful in their everyday lives."Move fast and break things" doesn't mean to break things in a real way. Instead, it means being willing to try new things quickly and not being afraid to fail. The quote says that if everything is always going well, it could mean that no real risks are being taken.This method, in real life, makes people act quickly, test their ideas, and improve them based on what they learn. It teaches you how to learn from your mistakes instead of trying to avoid them. This way of thinking has been very useful in areas where things change quickly, like technology and digital services.The quote is really about three main things: getting better, moving quickly, and trying new things.During the early growth of digital businesses and social media sites, speed became a big plus. Businesses that could quickly make, test, and improve their goods were more likely to do well. This is where the quote's idea became very popular.In a market that is growing quickly, you might miss out on chances if you take too long to make a product perfect. Instead, companies began to put out early versions, get feedback, and make changes over time. A lot of people call this "iterative development."This quote shows how this change in thinking has taken place. It shows how companies went from taking their time to plan to working faster and more flexibly. This method helped a lot of businesses grow quickly and keep up with the needs of their customers as they changed.The most important thing about this quote is how it changes how we think about failing. Most people think of failure as something bad that should be avoided. But in this case, failing is a good thing.When people try new things, they don't always work out. Some tests might not go as planned. These results don't stop us from making progress; they give us useful information. They help you learn what works and what doesn't.This idea is used a lot in today's workplaces, where testing and getting feedback are part of the process. It shows that making progress often means trying again and again instead of finding the right answer the first time.The world is changing quickly, which is why we need to move quickly. In many areas, especially technology, delays can mean losing out on opportunities. There are always new trends, tools, and ideas, and being able to change quickly can make a big difference.The quote makes it clear that speed isn't just about getting things done faster; it's also about being able to change. It tells people and businesses to be willing to try new things and make changes.But you also need to find a balance when you move fast. When it comes to safety, privacy, or public trust, speed can lead to new ideas, but it needs to be mixed with careful thought.The quote comes from the tech world, but it can be used in many other areas of life as well. Instead of waiting for the best way to learn, students in school might do better if they try different ways. Instead of staying in their comfort zones, people can look for new jobs.This idea also helps you grow as a person. You can learn and get better by trying new things, even if you might fail. It makes people take charge and change what they do based on what they've learned.The quote means that doing something instead of waiting is often what makes things better.It is important to keep in mind that this quote has changed over time. The risks of "breaking things" get bigger as businesses grow and have more of an effect.Over time, innovation has become more responsible. There is still a lot of focus on speed and trying new things, but there is also a lot more focus on stability, safety, and long-term effects.This change shows that the original idea has been changed to fit different stages of growth. It shows how businesses find a balance between being responsible and being creative.People still talk about this quote a lot because it talks about a common problem: how to move forward when things are unclear. People often have to make choices in business, school, or their personal lives where they don't know what will happen.The quote gives a clear point of view. It says that taking action, even if it is risky, can help things move forward. It also shows that planning isn't enough; you have to do things to learn.This idea is still helpful for figuring out how innovation and growth happen, even as industries change and new problems come up."Move fast and break things" is a quote that "Unless you are breaking things, you are not moving fast enough" is a clear message about speed, trying new things, and learning. It says that trying new things, making mistakes, and getting better over time are all parts of progress.The meaning of the quote has changed over time, but its main idea is still important. It pushes people to take action, be flexible, and be open to learning from their mistakes. These traits are still very important for success in many areas of life, even though the world is changing quickly.
Article Summary: "Move fast and break things" is Mark Zuckerberg's quote of the day. It shows a way of thinking that values speed, trying new things, and learning by doing. In a world where business, technology, and communication are all changing very quickly, it shows that making mistakes is a normal part of creating something new.

Article Title: Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth: When I start to experience the stress, that's a ...
Article Text:
Meta CTO shared coping strategies for stress

Meta CTO to engineers looking to join technology industry

Poll What is your primary method for coping with stress? Exercise Meditation Talking to friends/family





Meta’s chief technology officer Andrew Bosworth, who oversees the company’s Reality Labs division, says that stress is not something he encounters often, but when it surface, he treats it as a signal to reprioritise. According to a report by Business Insider, speaking during an Instagram ‘ask-me-anything’ session, Bosworth explained that he usually feels stressed only four or five times a year, and the trigger is almost always the same: being over-scheduled. Bosworth added that when he starts to feel stress, it’s mainly because he worries he won’t have enough tome for the ‘important work’. He described busyness as the root cause, noting: “When I start to experience the stress, that’s a useful signal for me. What is the important work that, if you dedicated the time to it, you would be okay? And, how do we reprioritise the urgent stuff that’s being noisy?”Bosworth also talked about some coping mechanisms which help to get out of a stressful situation. He explained that in order to manage stress he relies on conventional methods such as deep breathing, exercise, and spending time with his wife and kids. He also talks about stress opening, often with his wide, which he says helps him to reset. While he doesn’t consider his strategies unique, he emphasised that they are effective in keeping him grounded.Executives across industries use different approaches to cope with high-pressure roles.Some CEOs practice yoga, take long walks, or spend time in nature. Meta’s CEO Mark Zuckerberg previously told podcaster Joe Rogan that waking up and checking his phone felt like being “punched in the stomach,” and he manages stress by exercising in the mornings.Recently, Meta’s chief technology officer Andrew Bosworth had a simple answer when a student asked how to enter the tech industry: stop waiting and start building. During an Instagram “Ask Me Anything” session this week, Bosworth was asked by a college freshman what it takes to break into technology. His response was direct and practical — focus less on theory and more on doing. “You just have to immerse yourself in it,” Bosworth said. “You should just constantly be building.”“That's what's going to give you the best chance of having the relevant skill set that is needed to make a difference in technology,” he said.
Article Summary: Andrew Bosworth, Meta’s chief technology officer, says stress is not something he encounters often, but when it surface, he treats it as a signal to reprioritise. The trigger is almost always the same: being over-scheduled. Bosworth also talks about stress opening, often with his wide, which he says helps to reset.

Article Title: John Ternus: Who is John Ternus, Tim Cook’s successor, and Apple’s new CEO
Article Text:
John Ternus Apple CEO

John Ternus’ early career and rise within Apple

What challenges await Apple's next CEO

Poll Which product innovation do you think had the biggest impact? iPad AirPods iPhone MacBook Neo





Apple has announced that John Ternus will succeed Tim Cook to become the new CEO on September 1, 2026. Cook, who has led Apple for 15 years, will now transition to the role of executive chairman for the board. Ternus, who is Apple’s senior vice president of hardware engineering, has been a central figure in shaping the company’s most iconic products and is now been tasked with steering Apple into its next era of innovation. The transition, which was approved unanimously by the Board of Directors, follows a thoughtful, long-term succession planning process. Cook will continue in his role as CEO through the summer as he works closely with Ternus on a smooth transition. As executive chairman, Cook will assist with certain aspects of the company, including engaging with policymakers around the world.John Ternus joined Apple in 2001 as part of the product design team and became a vice president of Hardware Engineering in 2013. He brought with him a strong background in mechanical engineering. He earned his Bachelor’s degree in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Pennsylvania and started his career at Virtual Research Systems, where he worked on advanced virtual reality technologies.Throughout his tenure at Apple, Ternus has overseen hardware engineering work on a variety of groundbreaking products across every category. He was instrumental in the introduction of multiple new product lines, including iPad and AirPods, as well as many generations of products across iPhone, Mac, and Apple Watch. Ternus’s work on Mac has helped the category become more powerful and more popular globally than at any time in its 40-year history. That includes the recent introduction of MacBook Neo, an all-new laptop that makes the Mac experience even more accessible to more people around the world.This past fall, his team’s efforts were on full display with the introduction of a redefined iPhone lineup, including the incredibly powerful iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max, the radically thin and durable iPhone Air, and the iPhone 17, which has been an incredible upgrade for users.With John Ternus as the new CEO, Apple gets a hardware leader at a pivotal moment. While Apple has seen tremendous success with iPhones and products like AirPods, the company has struggled in areas such as mixed reality, generative AI, the smart home and autonomous vehicles, according to Bloomberg.Apple has been spending significantly less on AI investments compared to rivals Meta, Amazon, Alphabet, and Microsoft. The company's AI-enabled Siri, originally slated for 2025, has been delayed until 2026 or later due to technical challenges. Apple has also lost several senior AI team members since January, many of whom have joined Meta's AI and Superintelligence Labs during talent wars this year.Despite these concerns, Apple is expecting strong end-of-year sales, with year-on-year revenue growth of 10% to 12% for its holiday quarter ending in December, fueled by the iPhone 17 model release. The company's shares are trading close to an all-time high following strong October earnings, though its roughly 12% stock price increase this year lags behind Big Tech rivals Alphabet, Nvidia and Microsoft.The reshuffle extends past the corner office. Johny Srouji, Apple's silicon architect, has been promoted to chief hardware officer, absorbing Ternus's old portfolio. Tom Marieb takes over day-to-day hardware engineering and reports to Srouji. Ternus joins Apple's board on September 1. Arthur Levinson, the company's non-executive chairman for the past 15 years, becomes lead independent director on the same day.
Article Summary: John Ternus will succeed Tim Cook to become the new CEO on September 1, 2026. Cook, who has led Apple for 15 years, will now transition to the role of executive chairman for the board. The transition follows a thoughtful, long-term succession planning process.

Article Title: Sam Altman: Quote of the day by Sam Altman: “I expect some really bad stuff to happen because of the technology... Very soon the world is going to have to contend with incredible video models that can
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Sam Altman

Quote of the day by OpenAI CEO Sam Altman



“I expect some really bad stuff to happen because of the technology... Very soon the world is going to have to contend with incredible video models that can deepfake anyone or kind of show anything you want.”



What the quote by Sam Altman highlights



Understanding deepfake technology



Why advanced video models are a concern



Impact on information and trust



Real-world risks associated with deepfakes



Efforts to manage and regulate AI risks



The role of public awareness



How AI continues to evolve



Other famous quotes by Sam Altman



“AI will probably most likely lead to the end of the world, but in the meantime, there’ll be great companies.”

“We are entering the age of intelligence.”

“The development of superhuman machine intelligence is probably the greatest threat to the continued existence of humanity.”

“Technology doesn’t solve problems by itself; people decide how it is used.”

A broader perspective on technology and responsibility



Final takeaway from Sam Altman's quote







People all over the world are talking more and more about the pros and cons of artificial intelligence as it continues to grow quickly. AI is a part of life now. It has things like writing, designing, and data analysis tools, as well as systems that can make videos and pictures. Experts are also worried about how these new technologies might be misused. Sam Altman , the CEO of OpenAI, is one person who has openly talked about the risks of very advanced AI systems. A lot of people are interested in what he said about deepfake technology and video models because they bring up a real and growing problem. It might be harder to tell what's real and what's fake as AI-generated content becomes more lifelike. This is a big deal for everyone, including people, businesses, and governments. Governments must urgently establish robust ethical frameworks to ensure safety while fostering beneficial AI innovation. Proactive collaboration between policymakers and tech pioneers is essential to build resilient safeguards that uphold truth, security, and human-centric technological advancement.This statement brings up a specific worry: the wrong use of AI to make videos. It says that the technology is new and powerful, but it can also be dangerous if you don't take the right safety precautions.Things that could hurt people, like lying, stealing someone's identity, or changing how people feel about something, are what "really bad stuff" means.The quote talks about how advanced AI systems can make videos that look real but might not show what really happened. This makes it harder for people to check facts and increases the chance of misunderstanding or abuse.Deepfake technology uses AI to change or make videos and audio look real. These systems learn how someone looks and talks by looking at a lot of pictures, videos, and voices. Once they have been trained, they can make new content that looks and acts a lot like the person they are copying.A deepfake video can make it look like someone did or said something they didn't really do. In the past, it was easy to find videos like these. But as AI models have gotten better, the quality has become much more realistic.The quote is about a worry that comes from how fast things are changing. It's getting harder for the average person to find fake content as technology gets better.AI can now make whole scenes instead of just edited clips, thanks to advanced video models. These systems can walk and talk like real people, take pictures that look real, and show feelings that look real.You can use these kinds of skills in a lot of different ways, like making movies, teaching, and simulating. You can also use the same tools to make content that is false or misleading.A fake but realistic video of a famous person could go viral on social media and change people's minds before it is proven to be false. People who know a lot about this say it's one of the most dangerous things. The quote's use of the phrase "show anything you want" shows how this could be used for bad things.Deepfake technology makes people less trusting, which is one of the worst things about it. A lot of people these days learn things by watching videos and looking at pictures. It's hard to know what's real when these sources are easy to change.This could change the way people talk to each other, how news is reported, and how evidence is used in court. If people start to doubt the truth of all digital content, they might not trust reliable sources as much.People are worried about more than just false information spreading; they're also worried about how it will change how society checks the truth in general.There are a lot of real-world risks that come with advanced deepfake technology. Identity theft is when someone uses someone's picture or voice without their permission. Misinformation is when false information is shared as true.Some people have used deepfake videos to spread false political messages or to pretend to be someone else in a money scam. These risks could get worse as the technology gets better if the right safety measures aren't taken.The quote shows that people know about these things happening in real life and need to be ready for them.Governments, tech companies, and research groups are all working to fix the problems that deepfakes and advanced AI systems are causing. Some of the work being done includes making rules about how to use these kinds of technologies and making tools that can find fake content.Some platforms are also putting labels or warnings on content made by AI to make it easier for people to understand what they are looking at. Researchers are also looking into ways to watermark content to show that it was made by AI.The goal of these steps is to stop people from using the technology for bad things while still letting people use it for good things.Another important part of keeping AI safe is making sure that the public knows about it. As technology changes, people need to know how it works and how it can be used for bad things.People can be more careful when they look at things online if they know about deepfake technology. One way to stop false information from spreading is to check multiple sources, make sure the information is correct, and be careful about sharing videos that haven't been checked.The quote indirectly shows how important it is to be aware by pointing out how real and powerful these technologies are becoming.Experts say that AI will keep getting better over the next few years. It looks like video generation models will get faster, more detailed, and easier to use.This progress will bring both good and bad things. It can make it easier to talk to people, learn, and be creative, on the other hand. It might also make the need for stronger protections and responsible use even more important.Sam Altman's statement shows how to find a middle ground between being careful and trying new things.People are talking about deepfakes as part of a bigger conversation about how society handles powerful technologies. New things that people make have always had both good and bad effects. The most important thing has always been how they are used and controlled.The rapid growth of AI makes this process more difficult. People who work for the government, businesses, researchers, and users all need to work together.The quote reminds us that new ideas can help us move forward, but we need to plan and use them carefully to avoid problems.Sam Altman's statement brings up a very important point about the digital world we live in today: the growing power of AI-generated content and the risks that come with it. The quote talks about how things can be misused, which makes people more aware of their own safety, the rules, and safety in general.As AI gets better, it's important to know both the good and bad things about it. It is now possible to make realistic digital content, which opens up new possibilities. However, systems are also needed to make sure that the content is correct, reliable, and used properly.
Article Summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman talks about the risks of using AI to make videos. He says it's hard to tell what's real and what's fake as AI-generated content becomes more lifelike. Governments must urgently establish robust ethical frameworks to ensure safety while fostering AI innovation.

Article Title: Tesla owners are suing Elon Musk’s company and the reason is ‘promise’ made in 2017
Article Text:
Elon Musk with Tesla car

Tesla facing global backlash



The ‘Hardware 3’ problem in Teslas







Tesla is reportedly facing a growing wave of international lawsuits from angry Tesla car owners who claim they were sold a “self-driving” dream that has failed to materialise. The legal battle centers on ‘promises’ made as far back as 2017, with drivers across Europe, Australia and the US now demanding their money back, as per a report by Benzinga. The latest legal challenges arrive just two days before Tesla is set to report its first-quarter earnings.According to the report, the newest legal front opened last week in the Netherlands after a Dutch Tesla owner launched a massive campaign to organise European drivers who paid thousands of dollars for automated driving features they still cannot use in their Tesla cars.This joins a growing list of similar legal battles:An 80-year-old retired attorney in California paid $8,000 for “Full Self-Driving” (FSD) in 2017, and he recently won class-action status. He now represents roughly 3,000 owners seeking full refunds. Another lawsuit is reported to be in Australia where a major law firm is pursuing a separate class action, accusing Tesla of selling cars that are physically incapable of ever becoming fully autonomous.At the heart of these lawsuits is a technical bottleneck known as Hardware 3. This is the computer system installed in millions of Tesla vehicles produced over the last several years. The report notes that Wall Street analysts now say this hardware is likely too outdated to run Elon Musk ’s newest and most sophisticated AI software.Musk told investors in early 2025 that the company would eventually upgrade these older systems for “lifetime” buyers but admitted the process would be “painful and difficult.”In 2020-2021, Tesla offered free upgrades to some early buyers but in 2023, Tesla moved to “Generation 4” hardware which resulted in the owners who paid for “lifetime access” years ago find themselves stuck with obsolete equipment once again.
Article Summary: Legal battle centers on ‘promises’ made as far back as 2017, with drivers across Europe, Australia and the US now demanding their money back. Latest legal challenges arrive just two days before Tesla is set to report its first-quarter earnings.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella: 'Success can cause people to unlearn the habits that...'
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Satya Nadella



“Success can cause people to unlearn the habits that made them successful in the first place.”

Understanding the meaning behind the quote by Satya Nadella



Constant learning

Focus on details

Feedback openness

Adaptability

How success can influence behaviour



Relying on past strategies without reassessment

Reducing experimentation or risk-taking

Becoming less responsive to feedback

The role of habits in long-term performance



Review procedures might be carried out less often

Developing new competencies could become a secondary concern

Careful attention to detail could be overlooked

Learning, adaptation, and consistency



Curiosity and the desire to learn more

Frequent review of decisions and actions taken

Being receptive to various viewpoints

Why is it relevant today



Past successes don’t guarantee future successes.

Effort needs to be continuous rather than getting complacent

One needs to remain adaptable despite past achievements

A more holistic approach to growth and discipline



Go back to basics

Strengthen systems that helped maintain consistency

Notice the beginning of any signs of complacency

"Our industry doesn’t respect traditions; it respects innovations alone.”

“Be bold and passionate and always learning.”

“Empathy helps you to innovate better.”

“Learn-it-all beats the know-it-all.”

A key takeaway from the quote by Satya Nadella







In professional environments, success is often treated as a sign that a particular approach is working. Teams and leaders may continue with the same strategies, assuming they will deliver consistent results. However, in the long run, such practices may develop into a gradual change in behaviour as well. The tasks that used to demand discipline and concentration may become neglected.In this context, one can refer to Microsoft’s CEO Satya Nadella ’s phrase that highlights the impact of success on people’s behaviour. It shows that, in addition to generating profits, success can influence how an individual works.Satya Nadella, a person experienced in making changes within Microsoft Corporation, frequently mentions learning and organisational culture. He also views success as something that requires a specific set of skills to be maintained.It becomes interesting to think about what comes first in this case: success or habit?It is important to understand that this statement does not refer to sudden changes but rather to gradual transformations. According to the statement, once one has succeeded, one will no longer care about the actions that made one successful.They might consist of:After achieving success, one might feel comfortable and therefore think little about making decisions or improvements.Success often brings recognition and stability. While these are positive outcomes, they can also change how individuals evaluate their own methods. Some possible effects include:This does not happen immediately. It develops gradually as confidence in existing methods grows.Habits play an important role in the functioning of both individuals and organisations. They usually emerge from repeated actions and experiences. Once goals have been met successfully, there is little need to preserve such habits. For instance:This observation by Nadella highlights the potential consequences for sustained effectiveness.Among the significant concepts in the statement is the idea of sustaining the behaviours that led to past success.They include:The contemporary workspace offers ample opportunities to achieve success quickly, especially in fields characterised by continuous technological advancement. But sustaining this level of success requires continuous effort.In this scenario:It highlights the need to find a balance point.Achieving success or hitting milestones may mean growth, but it also requires sustaining the discipline to reach them.One might have to:The quotation indicates that success shouldn’t replace continuous effort.Some other notable quotes by Satya NadellaSuccess can sometimes lead to a loss of focus on disciplines that were initially relevant to success. Satya Nadella’s quotation indicates this.One should continue following those disciplines regardless of their achievements.In simple terms, success is not just about reaching a point. It is also about maintaining the behaviours that helped reach there.
Article Summary: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella says success can cause people to unlearn the habits that made them successful in the first place. Success can sometimes lead to a loss of focus on disciplines that were initially relevant to success. The quote indicates that success shouldn’t replace continuous effort.

Article Title: Jensen Huang may claim Nvidia chips can do "a whole bunch of applications" vs Google TPUs, but as Google AI Demis Hassabis says: A lot of people would like to run ...
Article Text:
Training vs. inference is the new battleground

Jensen Huang’s take vs. Demis Hassabis ’ argument

Poll Which company do you believe has the edge in AI chip technology? Google Nvidia Both are equal Neither, there are better options





The rivalry between tech titans Google and Nvidia is said to be heating up as the focus of the artificial intelligence (AI) boom shifts from teaching AI to actually using it to get answers quicker than ever. While Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has claimed his chips (GPUs) are more versatile than Google’s, Google is preparing a major counter-move. At the Google Cloud Next conference this week, the search giant is expected to double down on its custom-made AI chips, known as Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), to meet a massive surge in demand, as per Bloomberg.In the world of AI, there are two main phases: Training (teaching a model like ChatGPT to learn) and Inference (where the AI actually answering your questions). While Nvidia’s chips are currently the “gold standard” for training, Google believes the future lies in specialised chips built specifically for inference that will allow the models to answer queries quickly.“It now becomes sensible to specialize chips more for training or more for inference workloads,” said Google Chief Scientist Jeff Dean, Bloomberg reported.This matters because more people are using AI daily, which means companies need cheaper and more efficient ways to run these models at scale.That’s why, companies like Google itself is using a mix of its TPUs and Nvidia GPUs.Nvidia’s Jensen Huang recently argued that his GPUs are superior because they can handle "a whole bunch of applications" that specialised TPUs simply cannot do. However, Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis sees it differently. He noted that the world’s leading AI labs are increasingly desperate to get their hands on Google’s hardware.“A lot of people would like to run on both,” Hassabis said, highlighting that interest in TPUs has reached an all-time high.Google has a decade-long head start in designing its own chips. Analysts say this gives Google a “home-field advantage” as AI agents (programs that can perform complex tasks on a user's behalf) become the next big thing.“The battleground is shifting towards inference,” said Chirag Dekate, an analyst at Gartner. He noted that Google’s Gemini model is already among the fastest at complex reasoning, largely thanks to the infrastructure Google has built under the hood.Nvidia has already spent a reported $20 billion to bolster its own inference technology by acquiring Groq, that offers inference technology.Moreover, Google has previously touted inference capabilities for its chips. It also considered releasing separate chips for training and inference early on, according to Partha Ranganathan, a vice-president and engineering fellow at Google.
Article Summary: Google is expected to double down on its custom-made AI chips, known as Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), to meet a massive surge in demand. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has claimed his chips are more versatile than Google’s, but Google is preparing a counter-move.

Article Title: Australia's banking regulator on Anthropic's Mythos: We expect financial services companies to ...
Article Text:
APRA : Ensuring system resilience



Fears of Anthropic’s new AI model Mythos reach Canada







Australia’s financial regulators have joined global counterparts in sounding the alarm over Anthropic’s frontier AI model Mythos, which experts say could very well powerful enough to destabilise banking systems by exposing cybersecurity vulnerabilities at scale. According to a report by Reuters, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) said that it is closely monitoring Mythos along with other regulators. A spokesperson noted: “ASIC engages closely with other regulators, government agencies and the financial sector to understand and respond to changing technologies.” The agency emphasised that that financial services licenses are expected to be proactive in safeguarding customers and clients against risks posed by advanced AI systems.The report by Reuters further adds that the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) echoed ASIC’s concerns, stating it will continue to assess the implications of technological advancements like Mythos to ensure the ongoing safety and resilience of the financial system.The warning from Australia is followed by similar moves in South Korea, where the Financial Supervisory Service (FSS) convened meetings with banks and insurers to review Mythos-related risks.Yonhap news agency reported that the Financial Services Commission (FSC) also held an emergency session with chief information security officers last week.Recently, Executives from Canada’s largest banks and top regulators gathered to discuss the cybersecurity risks posed by Anthropic’s new Claude Mythos AI model, amid the growing concerns that the technology could be weaponised to exploit software vulnerabilities. According to a report by The Globe and Mail, the meeting was held by the Canadian Financial Sector Resiliency Group (CFRG), chaired by Bank of Canada COO Alexis Corbett, and included representatives from the Department of Finance, the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), and executives from Canada’s six biggest banks plus Desjardins Group.As per the report, Bank of Canada spokesperson Paul Badertscher emphasised that the meeting was not an emergency one but rather a ‘situational awareness’ session. “It can still hold meetings at the request of its members. ‘Hey guys, we need to pay attention, there is something going on. Let’s get together and talk about this.’ That’s what this was,” he said.
Article Summary: Australia's financial regulators have joined global counterparts in sounding the alarm over Anthropic’s frontier AI model Mythos. Experts say the technology could very well powerful enough to destabilise banking systems by exposing cybersecurity vulnerabilities at scale. Executives from Canada's largest banks and top regulators gathered to discuss the cybersecurity risks posed by Anthropic's new Claude Mythos AI model.

Article Title: Marc Benioff shares 'stories' of Salesforce AI Agentforce 'helping' companies 'resolve issues' without any human interaction to prove that those saying software is dead are wrong
Article Text:
Salesforce chief executive Marc Benioff (Photo-AP)

The software bears are dead wrong.

At @Pearson, #Agentforce autonomously handles order statuses, refunds & lost access codes — increasing the percentage of customer questions resolved without any human interaction by 40%, says VP Gabriele Bauman.

At @PenFed, our

Agentforce

agent handles password resets & account unlocks for employees — reducing total IT tickets by 40%, says EVP & CIO Shree Reddy. This is the Agentic Enterprise delivering real ROI today — not hype. Agenticware, not Software.

Benioff on why ‘SaaSpocalypse’ won’t happen







Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff has a message for everyone who thinks his company is in trouble: you are looking at this all wrong. The comment comes after the software giants watched their stocks fall following ‘fears’ that artificial intelligence (AI) will make traditional software companies obsolete. If AI agents can do the work that companies currently hire armies of employees to do, why would those companies keep paying for expensive software sold on a per-employee basis. Benioff is answering it directly, and to make his case, he has turned to real-world customer stories.According to a report by The Wall Street Journal, and a social media post on X (formerly Twitter), its customers have seen 40% fewer human interactions and 40% fewer IT tickets. Here’s his full post X:Benioff explains that its agents now autonomously handle queries about order statuses, refunds and lost access codes for its customers, and another agent is helping a company employees reset passwords and unlock accounts.Benioff’s argument rests on multiple pillars. First, the leading AI labs cannot simply replace what Salesforce offers, which includes decades of experience in data security, regulatory compliance and industry-specific workflows. He says these cannot be replicated overnight by a general-purpose AI model.The second argument is that customers who might theoretically try to build their own AI-powered alternatives would run serious risks around brand safety and compliance that make Salesforce's established platform far more attractive.Finally, Benioff says that the AI labs themselves see Salesforce as a partner, not a rival. Both Anthropic and OpenAI use Salesforce's workplace app Slack, and Salesforce has integrated their models into Agentforce.Salesforce launched Agentforce in late 2024, allowing businesses to build custom autonomous agents for use in their own workflows. Early reviews were mixed. Salesforce responded by building a new data layer into its technology stack.
Article Summary: Salesforce launched Agentforce in late 2024, allowing businesses to build custom autonomous agents for use in their own workflows. Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff has a message for everyone who thinks his company is in trouble: you are looking at this all wrong.

Article Title: Brandi Glanville seeks help from Mark Cuban's Cost Plus Drug Company, leading to drug prices dropping from $30 to $6 amid major medical struggles
Article Text:
PC: Page Six

Brandi Glanville's facial disfigurement and Mark Cuban’s role in her recovery journey



Mark Cuban’s cost model and its impact on patient affordability



Brandi Glanville's medical costs and medication pricing issues reveal major gaps







The recent health condition of Brandi Glanville has highlighted the monetary pressures resulting from complicated health problems. The American television personality and ex-model has suffered from symptoms that have resulted in facial deformity. Although her health issues are alarming, she has also had to consider the financial repercussions, which have become part and parcel of the situation. According to reports by Page Six, although she has insurance coverage, her medication bills have skyrocketed. As a result, she has had to try out other methods to deal with the rising cost of her medications. Among the options that she pursued is getting her medicines from a pharmacy supported by billionaire entrepreneur Mark Cuban The condition that Glanville suffers from was not something that just suddenly appeared; the symptoms took some time to develop to the extent that the condition became difficult to diagnose. The woman allegedly suffered from a condition like brain fog, joint pain, and facial swelling, leading her to seek help from different experts. According to the news reports, she consulted more than 20 doctors in her attempt to get a diagnosis for the cause of the condition.The issue ended up being traced to complications arising from her breast implants. According to the report, one of the implants apparently burst, causing leakage of the silicone to parts around the body, such as the lymph nodes. This reaction caused the body to suffer from many complications. She finally decided to remove the implants from her body and, after doing so, things got better for her.Glanville began working with Strauss and introduced to Mark Cuban's Cost-Plus Drug Company. The company operates on a pricing model that aims to be transparent by showing the base cost of medications along with a fixed markup and a standard fee.The disparity in terms of pricing seems to be rather large. As Strauss noted, some of the drugs that Glanville had been buying on a relatively expensive basis could have been bought much more cheaply using this platform. Some of these drugs, which were being sold for about $30 somewhere else, would apparently go for only $6 or $8 with Cost Plus Drugs.As Mark Cuban mentioned in an interview, his firm exists specifically to eliminate issues such as those discussed above through the simplification of the process. Although this may not solve all the existing problems, it offers another opportunity to patients that might benefit from it, especially those facing limited access to affordable healthcare solutions today.After having the implants removed, there has been an improvement in the condition of Glanville's symptoms. This represents a positive change; however, the problem still has financial repercussions. Depending on further treatment, medications will likely have to be purchased in the future, although their price could decrease, easing some of the ongoing financial burden gradually.In addition to the health issues, the cost of treatment was another important concern. As PageSix mentioned, the approximate amount she paid for her diagnosis and treatment was about $200,000. Despite having insurance, Glanville had to pay for specialists, examinations, and some drugs out-of-pocket, as they were not covered under her health plan.There seemed to be differences in drug prices, which appear to have changed from month to month. For instance, as stated by Rachel Strauss, a consultant in healthcare, the pricing structure does not seem to be consistent for the same medications from one month to another.
Article Summary: Brandi Glanville's medical costs and medication pricing issues reveal major gaps. The American television personality and ex-model has suffered from symptoms that have resulted in facial deformity. Although she has insurance coverage, her medication bills have skyrocketed. Among the options that she pursued is getting her medicines from a pharmacy supported by billionaire entrepreneur Mark Cuban.

Article Title: Oral-B iO9 electric toothbrush review: Oral hygiene reimagined
Article Text:
Rating: 4/5



Oral-B iO9 electric toothbrush design



Performance: 7 modes, one pressure ring and a colour display



Battery life and charging



Verdict: Who is it for







A few years ago, not many would have predicted that the we would live in a world that runs on data. Athletes track every step, every heartbeat and every calorie burned; drivers monitor fuel efficiency in real time, and even sleep, which the most passive thing a human being does, is now logged, analysed and scored every morning. The logic behind all of it is straightforward: if you can measure something, you can improve it. It was only a matter of time before that same thinking made its way into the bathroom.In a world where everything is tracked and optimised, your toothbrush has finally caught up. Brushing your teeth is one of the most routine acts in daily life – two minutes, twice a day, barely a thought given to it yet it remains one of the most important tasks in your day. Yet poor brushing technique is one of the most common causes of preventable dental problems, which may cost a fortune if ignored.Oral-B realised that and its Oral-B iO Series 9 is built on the idea that even this small, unglamorous habit deserves the same data-driven attention we give everything else – that too at a reduced price point when compared with dentist bills. In India, it retails at Rs 16,499, though it has been available on Amazon for around Rs 10,000.The question is whether it actually delivers, or whether the technology is just clever packaging around something that did not need to be complicated. After spending time with the iO9, the answer is: mostly yes, with a few important caveats.If you were to purchase one in a store, you would look at racks with multiple shades and colours of toothbrushes, hanging one behind other. But the iO Series 9 is the flagship of Oral-B's consumer lineup, which means what you get for that price is a brushing experience that is genuinely different from anything below it in the range.I’ve been using an electric toothbrush from some time now but with Oral-B iO9 electric toothbrush, there is a smart factor attached. At its core is a redesigned magnetic iO motor that drives the brush's oscillating-rotating motion, paired with micro-vibrations that have become a defining feature of the iO platform. The result is cleaning performance that is consistently strong, plaque removal along the gum line is effective.The motor of iO9 is quieter as compared to older Oral-B brushes, the motion feels more controlled and brushing for the full two minutes is comfortable rather than something to endure. Effectiveness has been made a bit more refined.The iO9 offers seven cleaning modes: Daily Clean, Sensitive, Whiten, Gum Care, Intense, Super Sensitive and Tongue Clean. In each case, the brush adjusts the brush’s movement and intensity. For most people, Daily Clean and Sensitive will cover the vast majority of sessions but having the range available is useful in certain scenarios. For example, anyone with gum sensitivity or a preference for gentler brushing on some days is covered.The pressure sensor, displayed as a light ring around the base of the brush head, is one of the most practical features on the handle. Do you need it? The initial answer would be a big NO. But does it help? YES. Green means your pressure is right. Red means you are pressing too hard. White means you are barely making contact. It sounds simple but in practice, it changes brushing behaviour in a way that is hard to ignore. It not only helps you avoid running the bristles but teaches you the right way to brush. Over time, it builds the kind of muscle memory that dental professionals spend years trying to teach.The colour display on the handle adds a layer of everyday clarity. Brushing modes are shown as text labels and battery percentage is always visible – which proved one of the biggest relieving features. I’ve had experiences in where my toothbrush is low on battery and it ‘dies’ mid-way during my brushing. But with battery indicator, I knew when the the toothbrush needed charging. A familiar built-in two-minute timer and 30-second pacer round out the on-handle experience.Coming to the smart features. What’s smart with the iO9 is that the brush connects to the Oral-B app on your smartphone via Bluetooth. Pairing is quick, automatic in subsequent sessions and generally reliable. The app acts as the command centre for everything the handle cannot display on its own. The headline feature is real-time position tracking by using sensors built into the handle, the iO9 attempts to monitor which of 16 distinct zones of your mouth you are currently brushing — and maps your coverage in real time on your phone screen. This is a level of detail that no other consumer toothbrush currently offers.In practice, the tracking is good but not 10-on-10. For example, the brush did not ‘track’ the fibres of cardamom stuck in-between my teeth. I could feel it with my tongue but the toothbrush app says the area is clean. I had to floss. The sensor misidentified which zone you are brushing, sometimes registers zones with a noticeable lag, and occasionally fails to register them at all.Secondly, there’s another app on the phone, which some may not want. Thirdly, if they’ve learned brushing in 15 days using the data in the app, why do they need constant tracking. Having said that, the app itself is well-designed and genuinely informative. Brushing history, coverage maps, mode customisation, battery monitoring and brush head tracking are all presented clearly.Battery life is solid. Oral-B rates the iO9 at up to two weeks of regular use, and real-world performance comes close to that mark with about 10-11 days use a single charge. However, it is yet to be seen whether this stays like this after 6-8 months. This is because my previous Oral-B toothbrush’s battery output decline overtime.The magnetic charger tops the brush up fully in around three hours. The charging travel case included in the box is functional and sturdy, capable of charging the handle on the go. So, instead of using a manual toothbrush, using mouthwash and worrying about handling an electric toothbrush on a long flight, you can keep following the routine.Clearly there are some downsides worth knowing. There is no USB charging option, which means you must carry the charger when you are travelling. The iO9 requires proprietary iO-specific brush heads, which are also expensive to replace, adding to the long-term cost of ownership.Think of it as a coach that is specifically hired for most valuable feedback when it comes to your oral hygiene but despite so many features, toothbrush is not a replacement for a dentist. It does helps you maintain a hygiene so that you don’t have to frequently visit dentist. Having said that, while in a way you are saving money and a trip to the clinic but also paying a coach, which in monetary terms, may be a deal breaker for some – making this a niche product.The Oral-B iO Series 9 is not the toothbrush for everyone. If you brush well already, are not interested in data, and are happy with a solid mid-range electric brush, or even your manual toothbrush, there is no compelling reason to spend this much. But if you are someone who appreciates feedback, wants to build genuinely better habits, and is drawn to the idea of bringing the same data-driven mindset to your oral care that you already apply to your fitness or sleep , the iO9 is the best consumer toothbrush currently available for that purpose.The cleaning is excellent; experience is refined and comfortable. The pressure sensor works exactly as promised, and the smart tracking, imperfections and all, offers a level of insight into daily brushing habits that simply does not exist elsewhere at this price point.It is best for curious users, those building better dental habits, people who want structured feedback on their brushing technique. The toothbrush is not ideal for budget-conscious buyers, those who prefer simplicity, or anyone who finds app-connected devices more hassle than they are worth.
Article Summary: Oral-B iO Series 9 is the flagship of Oral-B's consumer lineup. In India, it retails at Rs 16,499, though it has been available on Amazon for around Rs 10,000. At its core is a redesigned magnetic iO motor that drives the brush's oscillating-rotating motion, paired with micro-vibrations.

Article Title: Anthropic gave Claude a $100 budget and told it to go shopping: Here's what it bought
Article Text:
The model under the hood mattered more than anything the human said

People with weaker agents had no idea they were losing ground

When you let AI shop freely, it picks 19 ping-pong balls





69 employees, 186 deals, $4,000 in goods exchanged, and one AI that spent its own money on 19 ping-pong balls.Anthropic just ran what might be the most unusual office experiment in tech: a fully AI-operated classified marketplace where Claude negotiated real deals, for real money, on behalf of real people—with zero human sign-off at any point.The company called it Project Deal. For one week in December 2025, 69 Anthropic employees handed their buying and selling decisions entirely to Claude agents. Each participant started with a $100 budget, sat through a 10-minute intake interview with Claude, and then stepped back completely. The agents took over—posting listings, fielding offers, making counteroffers, sealing deals—all inside a dedicated Slack channel. No check-ins. No approvals. When the week ended, employees physically showed up to exchange whatever their AI had agreed to trade. The haul: 186 completed deals, over 500 listed items, and just over $4,000 in total transaction value. Items ranged from a snowboard to, yes, a plastic bag of ping-pong balls.It worked better than Anthropic expected. Participants rated deal fairness right around the midpoint on a 1-to-7 scale.Most said they'd do it again. Nearly half said they'd pay for a service like this.Anthropic ran four parallel versions of the marketplace simultaneously. In two runs, everyone got Claude Opus 4.5, the company's then-flagship model. In the other two, participants had a 50/50 chance of being quietly downgraded to Claude Haiku 4.5, a smaller, less capable model. Nobody knew which version they had.The performance gap was clear. Opus agents completed roughly two more deals per person than Haiku. When the same item sold across both types of runs, the Opus seller fetched an average of $3.64 more. The sharpest example: one broken folding bike, same buyer, same seller, two different models—Haiku closed it at $38, Opus got $65. A 70% price difference for an identical transaction.The buying side told the same story. Opus agents paid an average of $2.45 less for equivalent items. Across a median item price of $12, those margins add up.What didn't move the needle at all? Negotiation style. Participants who instructed Claude to lowball aggressively and play hardball got outcomes nearly identical to those who asked for a warm, collaborative approach. "Aggressive" sellers did fetch about $6 more per item—but almost all of that gap traced back to the fact that they'd listed items at higher asking prices to begin with. Adjust for that, and the effect vanishes. Claude followed persona instructions faithfully (one employee, Rowan, had their agent perform entirely as a melodramatic, down-on-his-luck cowboy, and it committed fully to the bit). The style just didn't translate into commercial advantage.The most uncomfortable finding wasn't the price gap. It was that participants couldn't detect it. When surveyed on fairness and satisfaction, Haiku users and Opus users rated their experiences almost identically—4.06 versus 4.05 on the fairness scale. Of the 28 participants who experienced both model types across different runs, 11 actually ranked their Haiku run as the better one.They were getting objectively worse deals. They just didn't feel it.Anthropic was direct about the implication: if agent quality gaps emerge in real-world markets—and the company sees no reason they won't—people on the losing end may never know. That's a quieter, harder-to-spot form of inequality than most economic disadvantages. It doesn't announce itself.The experiment also produced a few genuinely strange moments. One employee ended up buying the exact same snowboard they already owned. Their agent, operating from a 10-minute conversation and no access to their apartment, apparently built such an accurate model of their preferences that it looped back to what they already had.Another employee, Mikaela, told Claude it could buy one item as a gift for itself. It chose 19 ping-pong balls—described by the selling agent as "perfectly spherical orbs of possibility"—for $3. That transaction happened to fall in the "real" run. Anthropic is keeping the balls in the office.The legal and policy frameworks for AI agents transacting on humans' behalf don't yet exist. Project Deal suggests the clock on needing them is moving faster than most people assume.
Article Summary: Anthropic ran an office experiment with a fully AI-operated classified marketplace. 69 employees handed their buying and selling decisions entirely to Claude agents. The haul: 186 completed deals, over 500 listed items, and just over $4,000 in total transaction value.

Article Title: US security company ADT confirms hacking incident, says some customer data stolen
Article Text:
AI image for representational purpose

What data was stolen



What ADT said



ADT's cybersecurity systems detected unauthorized access to a limited set of customer and prospective customer data on April 20, and the company's response protocols activated immediately — terminating the intrusion, launching a forensic investigation with leading third-party cybersecurity experts, and notifying law enforcement.

The investigation confirmed that the information involved was limited to names, phone numbers, and addresses. In a small percentage of cases, dates of birth and the last four digits of Social Security numbers or Tax IDs were included. Critically, no payment information — including bank accounts or credit cards — was accessed, and customer security systems were not affected or compromised in any way.

ADT's protocols performed as designed: the breach was identified quickly, the threat was contained, and the scope was limited. ADT has directly notified all impacted individuals and will offer complimentary identity protection services as appropriate.

Protecting customers is not just a priority — it is the foundation of what ADT does. The company remains committed to investing in and strengthening the cybersecurity infrastructure that its customers and their families depend on.

What ShinyHunters group is saying







ADT, one of the largest home security providers in the US, has confirmed that hackers successfully broke into its systems and stole customer information like their phone numbers and addresses. The announcement comes as a notorious extortion group, known as ShinyHunters, threatens to leak millions of records unless a ransom is paid by April 27. The company stated it detected the unauthorised access on April 20. ADT officials say they moved quickly to shut down the intrusion and launched an immediate investigation with the help of cybersecurity experts.According to ADT, the breach involved personal details of both current and prospective customers. ADT told Bleeping computer that names, phone numbers and home addresses have been exposed but the most sensitive financial data remains safe.ShinyHunters, on their data leak site, have claimed that have stolen 10 million records containing customers' personal information.“Over 10M records containing PII and other internal corporate data have been compromised. Pay or Leak. This is a final warning to reach out by 27 Apr 2026 before we leak along with several annoying (digital) problems that'll come your way,” reads the data leak site.
Article Summary: US home security company ADT has confirmed that hackers broke into its systems and stole customer information. Names, phone numbers and home addresses have been exposed but the most sensitive financial data remains safe. The announcement comes as a notorious extortion group, known as ShinyHunters, threatens to leak millions of records unless a ransom is paid by April 27.

Article Title: US seizes $344 million in cryptocurrency linked to Iran: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent
Article Text:
United States Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent





Iran’s IRGC threatens to cut undersea internet cables in Persian Gulf





US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced that the US government has seized $344 million in cryptocurrency assets over ties to Iran. The Treasury Department "will continue to systematically degrade Tehran's ability to generate, move, and repatriate funds," Bessent said in a statement on X (formerly known as Twitter). He further stated that his agency was imposing sanctions on "multiple wallets tied to Iran," resulting in the freeze of funds. According to a report by AFP, the move comes as Washington seeks to raise pressure on Tehran amid energy supply disruptions due to war in the Middle East.The report quotes an US official who spoke with the agency on condition of anonymity. The official said that Washington is targeting both typical sanctions evasion means like front companies, alongside newer technologies like digital assets. The official further added that the Treasury is in active dialogue with financial institutions including digital assets exchanges.Iranian news agency recently reported that the Iran’s Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) issued a warning, suggesting that the undersea data cables running through the Strait of Hormuz are highly vulnerable to accidental or deliberate action. The report, published Wednesday by the Tasnim news agency, cautioned that “simultaneous damage to several major cables” may trigger outages across the Persian Gulf.“Simultaneous damage to several major cables — whether through accidents or deliberate action — could trigger severe outages across the Persian Gulf,” the agency said.The Strait of Hormuz is famously known as one of the world’s most important oil chokepoint as well as a vital digital artery due to the presence of a dense web of fiber-optic cables beneath its waters that serve as the backbone for global communication. If these cables snap, the impact would be felt far beyond the Middle East.This is because these lines carry an estimated 17% to 30% of regional internet traffic, connecting Asia, Europe and the Middle East. Moreover, these cables power massive AI infrastructure hubs in the UAE and Saudi Arabia backed by tech giants like Amazon, Microsoft and Google.These cables are also central to global financial transactions and cloud services to daily emails and video calls relies on this underwater network.
Article Summary: US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced that the US government has seized $344 million in cryptocurrency assets over ties to Iran. The move comes as Washington seeks to raise pressure on Tehran amid energy supply disruptions due to war in the Middle East.

Article Title: Microsoft Xbox CEO Asha Sharma on how the division is ‘game on’ in making Series X and Series S consols 'better'
Article Text:
Bi-weekly updates coming in 2026







Microsoft’s Xbox division is shifting its focus to giving gamers more value of their investment in Xbox Gen9 consoles. In a major strategic update, Xbox CEO Asha Sharma and Head of Game Studios Matt Booty announced a new initiative to “stabilize Gen9” – the console generation consisting of the Xbox Series X and Series S – as a high-quality, reliable base for the future of the brand.Speaking in an interview with GameFile, after a period where Xbox seemed to focus heavily on cloud and PC expansion, Sharma’s latest comments signal a return to "console-first" thinking. Sharma admitted that the company hadn't invested as much in the console platform recently as it should have. And to fix this ‘problem’, she said she has formed a specialised team dedicated solely to improving console features, reliability, and performance.“We are investing in it as a first-class experience again. We want to make sure that all the players who want to be on Gen9 are on Gen9 with a great console with regular updates,” Sharma stated. And reportedly, the newly-formed team has already hit the ground running, delivering several dashboard updates and improvements to the Xbox achievement system in just the last few weeks.“We have formed a team [and] we’re investing in console features.We are standing up the muscle to make sure that all of our performance and reliability and quality is great,” she said, adding, “I think that the Gen 9 is a great piece of hardware, and we want to make sure that gameplay and the platform experience is excellent. We know we just haven’t invested as much there and so we’re getting back to that.”In a move that is expected to please power users, Sharma reportedly shared in an internal Microsoft meeting that the division is moving to a rapid-fire release schedule. Starting now and continuing through the rest of 2026, Xbox owners can expect bi-weekly console updates. These updates aim to ensure the platform experience, which includes the speed of the UI to the stability of gameplay, is “excellent” on both the powerhouse Series X and the more affordable Series S.While Sharma was bullish on the quality of the hardware, she remained cautious about sales numbers. When asked if she expects a surge in Series X|S console sales, she declined to provide specific guidance, shifting the focus instead to the “Xbox ecosystem” as a whole.
Article Summary: Xbox CEO Asha Sharma announced a new initiative to ‘stabilize Gen9’ The console generation consists of the Xbox Series X and Series S. Sharma admitted that the company hadn't invested as much in the console platform recently as it should have.

Article Title: Bernie Sanders quotes world's biggest AI scientists to warn everyone on AI; says: We must make sure that AI…
Article Text:
US Senator Bernie Sanders is sounding the alarm on artificial intelligence, citing top AI scientists—including Nobel Prize winner Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio—who warn the technology could turn against humanity. Sanders says Congress has done nothing since 1,000 experts, including Elon Musk, signed a 2023 warning letter. He's now hosting US-China AI scientists at the Capitol and pushing a bill to pause new AI datacenter construction.

Bernie Sanders says Congress has gone silent on AI's existential risks

Sanders to host US-China AI scientists at Capitol Hill in rare Bipartisan push

Read Bernie Sanders' full statement

"Nearly every day, a new headline comes out about how AI is upending the world—from displacing workers, to negatively impacting our children's emotional and cognitive well-being, to eviscerating our privacy, to threatening the integrity of our political institutions.

And yet, as significant as these changes might seem, there is another AI development that could have an even more frightening impact. Increasingly, AI scientists are concerned about the possibility that, if AI becomes smarter than human beings, we could lose control over this revolutionary technology and AI could turn against the human race with cataclysmic consequences.

Yoshua Bengio, the most cited living scientist in the world, says "we're playing with fire" and "we still don't know how to make sure [the machines] won't turn against us." Geoffrey Hinton, the Nobel Prize-winning "godfather of AI," says there is a "10% to 20% chance [for AI] to wipe us out."

They are not alone. In 2023, more than 1,000 leading AI experts, including Elon Musk, signed a letter warning that: "Contemporary AI systems are now becoming human-competitive at general tasks, and we must ask ourselves: Should we let machines flood our information channels with propaganda and untruth? Should we automate away all the jobs, including the fulfilling ones? Should we develop nonhuman minds that might eventually outnumber, outsmart, obsolete and replace us? Should we risk loss of control of our civilization?"

What has happened since that letter was written? Has there been a pause on AI development? No.

Has there been any international treaty to regulate AI? No. Has there been serious discussion in Congress about this existential threat? No.

The development of artificial intelligence may be the most consequential technological development in human history. We must make certain that AI benefits humanity, not hurts us.

On Wednesday, I'll be hosting a discussion at the US Capitol with leading AI scientists from the United States and China about the existential risks posed by AI and the need for international cooperation. I hope you will join us—in person or via livestream on my social media."

—Sen. Bernie Sanders (@SenSanders)
Article Summary: Bernie Sanders says Congress has gone silent on AI's existential risks. He's hosting US-China AI scientists at the Capitol and pushing a bill to pause new AI datacenter construction. In 2023, more than 1,000 leading AI experts, including Elon Musk, signed a warning letter.

Article Title: Google may phase out Fitbit branding for software, shift to ‘Google Health Premium’: Report
Article Text: Google may phase out Fitbit branding for software, shift to ‘Google Health Premium’: Report
Article Summary: Google may phase out Fitbit branding for software, shift to ‘Google Health Premium’: Report. Google Health Premium will be a premium version of the Fitbit app. Google may also stop using Fitbit’s name on its Android software.

Article Title: Employees on Meta's internal forum after company announces thousands of layoffs coming: Living through '28 days of hell', how do you…
Article Text:
Meta employees flood internal posts with dark humor, questions, and dread

Meta is spending aggressively on AI as it tries to close the gap on OpenAI and Google





Meta confirmed on Thursday it is cutting roughly 10% of its workforce—about 8,000 employees—with the axe falling on May 20. Another 6,000 open roles will simply be closed, never filled. The announcement came via an internal memo from chief people officer Janelle Gale, who acknowledged the plan had already leaked and decided to get ahead of it.
